## 

| Agent Gateway \- User Guide  |  | Purpose Define functionality of Agent Gateway within the context of Agent Cloud.  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Authors**: [Vaibhav Katkade](mailto:vaibhavkatkade@google.com)[Andres Guedez](mailto:aguedez@google.com)[Piyush Nigam](mailto:piyushn@google.com)[Megan Yahya](mailto:meganyahya@google.com)[Sebastian Grabski](mailto:sgrabski@google.com)[Shriram Rajagopalan](mailto:shriramr@google.com)[Arsalaan Sobhan](mailto:arsalaan@google.com) **Contributors**: [Arunkumar Jayaraman](mailto:jarun@google.com)[Anoop Vetteth](mailto:avetteth@google.com)[Mahesh Viveganandhan](mailto:viveganandhan@google.com)[Taylor Money](mailto:youngmoney@google.com)[John Murray](mailto:johnmurray@google.com)[Piyush Nigam](mailto:piyushn@google.com)\<Add yourself\> | **Status**: In-Review **Last revised**: Jan 25, 2026 **Self-Link**: [go/agent-gateway-ug](https://goto.google.com/agent-gateway-ug)   | **Context:**   [Agent Cloud Platform Concept Review](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1bALpiO3KE_7kQtZ1XHVJpGWUYEYKAL81nnWdg0RblI4/edit?slide=id.g36f35699d09_0_1268#slide=id.g36f35699d09_0_1268) [Agent Cloud MVP mocks](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1ZQtf-T8W7eR3yHPGZqkIwPhRDLbuJ7IQanchAaoNaDY/edit?usp=sharing&resourcekey=0-ozzb7dZzh8nj3_9HBP5QVA) [go/agent-cloud-overview](http://goto.google.com/agent-cloud-overview) [go/agent-cloud-mvp-ug](http://goto.google.com/agent-cloud-mvp-ug) [go/iap-mcp-auth](http://goto.google.com/iap-mcp-auth) [go/1n-authzx-agent-cloud](http://goto.google.com/1n-authz-agent-cloud)         [go/fsp:agentregistry-ug](http://goto.google.com/fsp:agentregistry-ug) [Agentic IAM MVP](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1QwQ1vT0205ONNTYh5UYJkUE3GSAAh040rZCmPhW6Tdw/edit?tab=t.0) [Agent Identity, AuthZ and Governance Strategy](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1O2NFhCzi3i141lP1-2QFZchYV9JG9hDfdVrPbLuncGE/edit?usp=sharing&resourcekey=0-VjL9E4UB0jiRot4sBJidVA) [go/vertex-agent-engine-observability](http://goto.google.com/vertex-agent-engine-observability)  |

## Overview

To fulfill their tasks, AI Agents need access to various capabilities including tools, data stores, and other agents. As organizations scale their AI initiatives, they face an exponentially growing challenge of connecting each agent to multiple tools, creating an M×N integration problem that significantly slows development and increases complexity. As deployments grow to hundreds of agents and thousands of tools, enterprises need a more scalable and manageable solution, while also ensuring consistent governance, policies, and observability for agentic interactions across various environments. Furthermore, the autonomous nature of agentic applications and the novel risks from agentic protocols like MCP exacerbate the need for all agentic interactions to be governed by appropriate agent security controls.  

**Agent Gateway** is a functional component of [Agent Cloud](http://go/agent-cloud-overview) that provides simple, secure, and governed connectivity across all user-agent, agent-to-agent and agent-to-tools interactions, including agents acting on behalf of an end-user.  Agent Gateway offers a flexible and comprehensive approach to managing agent interactions across a diverse set of Google Cloud & external agent runtimes and deployment models, ensuring security, governance, and simplified operations for AI applications. Agent Gateway enables agents on Google Cloud or agentic applications from Google (e.g. Gemini Cloud Assist, Gemini CLI, etc.) or others (e.g. Cursor, Claude Code, etc.)  to securely interact with Tools, AI Models or other agents running anywhere. Agents running on a wide range of agent runtimes on Google Cloud such as Cloud Run, GKE, Gemini Enterprise and Vertex AI Agent Engine can use Agent Gateway to securely connect to Tools and other agents. All of these functionalities are available to agents irrespective of the agent development framework or client framework that is used, allowing developers to innovate easily and rapidly, while also providing consistent and comprehensive administration for enterprise platform and security teams. 

![][image1]

## Key Use-cases 

There are 2 main use-cases that Agent Gateway addresses: 

![][image2]

* **Use-case 1 – Secure and govern Agent interactions (Agent-to-Anywhere):** Agent Gateway provides administrators the ability to specify security and access management controls for agents accessing tools or other agents, and obtain consistent observability agentic interactions in their environment. These include access control policies for agentic protocols such as MCP. These controls are enforced in a supported set of Google Cloud products such as Cloud Load Balancers and Secure Web Proxies as a transparent forward proxy. 

* **Use-case 2 – Publish agents & tools (Client-to-Agent)**: Agent Gateway allows administrators to easily specify functions needed to securely publish agents and tools to clients, and instantiate them on a supported set of Google Cloud products such as Cloud Load Balancers. 

Agent Gateway supports a broad range of agent runtimes of Google Cloud such as GKE, Cloud Run, Gemini Enterprise and Vertex AI Agent Engine, as well as multi-cloud agents and tools deployments – including agentic clients such as Cursor, Claude Code, Gemini CLI, etc; and remote MCP servers. 

**Use-case 1 example: Internal AI Agent → External (Remote) MCP Server**   
Customer (e.g. Snap) has developed an in-house agent that connects to the [GitHub MCP servaer](https://github.com/github/github-mcp-server/blob/main/docs/remote-server.md) (3P) to analyze daily code check-ins. 

* Agent and the MCP Server are registered in [Agent Registry](http://go/fsp:agentregistry-ug). Administrators are able to define a GCP IAM policy that allows the agent-identity (using the Oauth client ID as principal) to be able to access the GitHubMCP server.   
  * Administrators can define a default permit/deny policy for access to unknown MCP servers.   
* Administrators are able to define a GCP-IAM Policy that only allows the agent to access the “Read-Only” MCP Tools based on the MCP Tools annotations. The MCP tools/call to read-write MCP Tools are blocked if such a  “Read-Only” MCP Tool policy is configured.   
* \[**Internal User → external MCP server example**\] In the future, this agent could be allowed to apply fixes and do GitHub code check-ins based on developer consent , which requires the developer to do an Oauth authentication/authorization \+ consent using their corporate credentials.  The agent gets a scoped **access token** that is tied to the user's identity and permissions and can only use this token to make calls to other services within the granted scope.

**Use-case 2 example: Internal User/Agent → Self-Hosted MCP Server**   
A customer (e.g. Uber) has approved agentic coding for a certain set of agentic tools (e.g. Gemini CLI, Cursor, VSCode, etc.) for their internal developers. [Figma MCP server](https://www.figma.com/blog/introducing-figmas-dev-mode-mcp-server/) (2P) is one of the MCP Servers that Uber has self-deployed on GCP (e.g. on a Cloud Run instance) to enable their front-end developers to do agentic coding.  Sam is a front-end developer in Uber using GeminiCLI for agentic coding. 

* Uber wants to ensure that only corporate employees that are developers can access this Figma MCP server through approved clients (like Gemini CLI). Uber is using Okta as its workforce identity authentication system. A client request to the Figma MCP server is authenticated using GCP-IAP, including using federated identities like Okta for the workforce authentication.   
* The enterprise administrator is able to define a GCP IAM policy that defines the approved agents/clients (E.g.  Gemini CLI, Cursor) that are able to access the Figma MCP Server.  If a non-registered client/agent tries to connect to that Figma MCP Server, then the access is blocked.   
  * The enterprise admin can define some other more fine-grained controls like “only allow access to the Read-only MCP Tools in Figma MCP” based on the MCP Tools annotations, or, only allow access to “Figma MCP server” from GCP projects that are tagged as “dev-environment”.   
* \[**Internal AI Agent → Self-Hosted MCP Server example\]** Uber has developed an autonomous internal agent that periodically makes calls to an internally developed MCP server for ride reviews to create a trust and safety report. The enterprise admin team needs to ensure authenticated, trusted access between this internal agent and the MCP server for ride reviews.


## Key features 

Agent Gateway provides the following features for agentic protocols such as Model Context Protocol (MCP) and Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol:

* **Authentication/Authorization**: You can use Agent Gateway to define authentication and authorization and govern which tools/other agents can be accessed by your agents; or which clients can access your agent. By default, Agent Gateway allows you to use Google Cloud’s Identity Aware Proxy and GCP IAM policies to implement access permissions to Tools and Agents.  You can enable authenticated access for:   
* **Internal or external agents** based on the SPIFFE identities, OAuth client IDs or JWT tokens and customizable authorization with enterprise authorization systems. This allows attribute-based access control over the agents or clients that can connect to the deployed agent or MCP server.      
* **Internal developers** in your organization, using their corporate credentials and choice of workforce identity solution (e.g. Okta, Entra, etc).   
* **External users**, authorized based on integration with the enterprise customer identity & access management systems, supporting methods like API Keys or Oauth   
* **Secure, optimized transport**: Agent Gateway allows you to define mutual TLS between agents-to-agents and agent-to-tools based on the agents and MCP servers identities with strong cryptographic attestation. Agent Gateway handles the mTLS handshake and termination ensuring authenticated, trusted and encrypted connectivity for your agent-agent and agent-tools connectivity without any developer overhead. Agent Gateway supports gRPC for low-latency, bi-directional streaming of traffic between agents and tools.   
* **Governed access to Tools and Agents:** Agent Gateway, as a part of Agent Cloud, allows enterprise administrators to enforce [access policies](#heading=h.ouh8vls4dw1o) to Tools and agents. These could be:  
  * Tools from MCP servers that are created and hosted by GCP customers themselves for their developers \[“2P MCP Servers”\] , or,    
  * Remote MCP servers from third-parties (e.g. GitHub, Stripe, Slack, Atlassian etc.) \[“3P MCP Servers”\]  
  * Their APIs as Tools 

  Enterprise administrators can enforce access policies to Tools or other agents based on their Google Cloud project hierarchy, the agent identity and role, environment attributes (e.g. Production vs Development) and Tools attributes (e.g. Read-Only). 

* **AI Security:** Agent Gateway allows you to specify AI runtime security protections to be applied in your network proxies to provide protections against agentic security risks like MCP prompt injection attacks. By default, you can use Google Cloud’s Model Armor, or supported 3P solutions like Palo Alto Networks AIRS for AI security. Additionally, you can also define controls such as GCP Cloud Armor to secure your agent or Tools deployment.   
* **Observability**: Agent Gateway facilitates improved observability of agentic interactions by providing Cloud Logging, Cloud Trace (supporting OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions) for MCP and A2A.

## Deployment Models  

Agent Gateway support two deployment modes: 

1. **Google-managed**:  You create an agent gateway resource as a [declarative configuration](#configuration-of-agent-gateway-resource) in your Google Cloud project, accessible over a Private Service Connect endpoint. The appropriate underlying network proxy is automatically created and managed by Google. This is useful when you want to manage minimal infrastructure, and minimal requirements to customize networking configurations. 

2. **Self-Managed**: You create an agent gateway resource as a [declarative configuration](#configuration-of-agent-gateway-resource), and attach it to an supported Google Cloud network proxy in your Google Cloud project. This is useful when you already have cloud networking infrastructure on Google Cloud that you want to extend for agentic governance functionality.   
   1. To govern client-to-agent connectivity – use a Google Cloud L7 Internal Load Balancer or GKE Gateway  
   2. To govern agent-to-any connectivity – use a Secure Web Proxy

## Configuration

### Configuration of Agent Gateway resource  {#configuration-of-agent-gateway-resource}

The Agent Gateway resource is the networking abstraction that enables AI-protocol awareness and the enforcement of AI safety, security and access control policies. It abstracts the underlying networking infrastructure, allowing users to define how agents communicate without managing the specific networking primitives.

Functionally, it can govern two paths:

1. **Agent-to-anywhere**: (Source) **Your agent** → (Destination) Model endpoints, APIs, Tools, Agents anywhere  
   An Agent Gateway can enforce access permissions and security guardrails for your agent interaction with any destination – agents, tools, models, APIs.

2. **Client-to-Agent**: (Source) Any client anywhere → (Destination) **Your agent**  
   An Agent Gateway can front-end your agent , in order to control client access to your agent and apply security guardrails for all client-to-agent interactions. 

YAML proto to define an Agent Gateway: 

| `### Agent Gateway resource definition name: my-agent-gateway  # List the protocol parsers to enable ; Only MCP supported in initial release protocols: [MCP]   ## A2A in post-MVP # Deployment mode of the network proxy  # GOOGLE_MANAGED - Proxy is orchestrated and managed by GoogleCloud in a tenant project # SELF_MANAGED - Use existing supported Google Cloud Networking product (e.g. SWP, Load Balancer, etc.)    #### IMMUTABLE (whole sub)  googleManaged:  # Operating Mode of Agent Gateway: 	# CLIENT_TO_AGENT - Protect inbound to Agent or Tool, or # AGENT_TO_ANYWHERE - Govern agent outbound to destinations  governedAccessPath: AGENT_TO_ANYWHERE | CLIENT_TO_AGENT   - self-managed:  # A supported Google Cloud networking proxy (e.g. L7-ILB/XLB, SWP, GKE Gateway) \\       # in the Project and Location  resource_uri = <gcp_network_proxy_resource_id>   # URI of Agent Registry - used to lookup governance attributes of registry entries  # Only applies in AGENT_TO_ANYWHERE path for initial release registries: ["//agentregistry.googleapis.com/projects/xxx/locations/xxx"]`     |
| :---- |
| `### Agent Gateway - Network Config definition  – Only needed for Google-managed Agent Gateway  network_config:    egress:      network-attachment: <>   # Optional - The PSC-Interface n/w attachment for connectivity to VPCs      dns_peering_config:         domain: <> # Required. Must end with a dot.         target_project: <>         target_network: <>` |

| Note: The \`network\_config\` field above is required only for short-term, and to be not required when PSC service binding and DNS binding (driven via Registry/AppHub) are available – ETA Q2/Q3’26 .  All these parameters can be created and updated using gCloud, Terraform and ADC templates.  |
| :---- |

This proto can be used consistently to define an Agent Gateway instance that can be used by any agent runtime (e.g. Agent Engine, Gemini Enterprise, GKE, CloudRun, agentic clients like Claude, etc.).  Run the following command to create an Agent Gateway based on the above YAML specification: 

```textproto
### Create the Agent Gateway   
gcloud network-services agent-gateways import my-agent-gateway  \\
  —-source=”my-agent-gateway.yaml”  \\
  [--location=<>]
```

Once the Agent Gateway is orchestrated, the Agent Gateway reflects the deployment details in an output-only field, the Agent Gateway Card.

```
gcloud network-services agent-gateway get my-agent-gateway 

Output:

// Output-only card for agents to connect to published endpoints.
// Common across Google-managed and self-managed deployment modes
agent_gateway_card: 
  - mTLS_endpoint: projects/<>/regions/<>/serviceAttachments/sa-name 
  - root_certificate: <>  // To be used as trust anchor by the agents to validate gateway
  - service-extensions-service-account: service-AGENTGW_PROJECT_NUMBER@gcp-sa-dep.iam.gserviceaccount.com  // For IAM permission
```

Note that Agent Gateway is registered to AppHub. It can be both a target attachment point for service binding as well as serve as a destination in service binding automation. The output-only card provides the required information for the agent side of routing to be set up against the published agent gateway endpoint. 

### Routing requests through Agent Gateway.  

#### **Gemini Enterprise**

In the Gemini Enterprise console, navigate to the Security page in your Gemini Enterprise application, and select the “Configure” tab. Once you have already created an Agent Gateway in Agent Platform, you can select it from the “Gateway Configuration” dropdowns.   
![][image3]

You can also select the Agent Gateway to use when creating a new Gemini Enterprise app :   
![][image4]

**Note**: 

* Only Gemini Enterprise agent calls to agents , tools and endpoints outside Gemini Enterprise go through Agent Gateway.   
  * In this release, agent-to-agent traffic for agents within Gemini Enterprise do not use Agent Gateway.   
  * Tool calls using Gemini Enterprise Integration Connectors do not use Agent Gateway.  
* Request from clients or users to Gemini Enterprise do not use Agent Gateway

#### **Agent Engine** 

To use Agent Gateway for agents on Agent Engine, pass in the Agent Gateway resource ID when creating a new Agent Engine instance in ADK as follows: 

```py
client_to_agent_gateway = (
    f"projects/{PROJECT_ID}/locations/us-central1/agentGateways/{CLIENT_TO_AGENT_GATEWAY_ID}"
)
agent_to_anywhere_gateway = (
    f"projects/{PROJECT_ID}/locations/us-central1/agentGateways/{AGENT_TO_ANYWHERE_GATEWAY_ID}"
)

# Deploy the agent with Agent Gateway
remote_app = client.agent_engines.create(
    agent=adk_app,
    config={
        "agent_gateway_config": {
            "client_to_agent_config": {"agent_gateway": client_to_agent_gateway},
            "agent_to_anywhere_config": {"agent_gateway": agent_to_anywhere_gateway},
        },
        "identity_type": types.IdentityType.AGENT_IDENTITY,
    },
)
```

As an Organization administrator , you can ensure that all Agent Engine instances use an Agent Gateway using a custom organization policy as follows: 

```py
# Restrict Agent Engine to Approved Agent Gateways

name: organizations/ORGANIZATION_ID/customConstraints/custom.requireEgressAgentGatwaysForAgentEngine
resourceTypes:
  - aiplatform.googleapis.com/ReasoningEngine
methodTypes:
  - CREATE
  - UPDATE
# Condition to DENY if Agent Gateway is NOT specified
condition: 
  !has(resource.spec.deploymentSpec.agentGatewayConfig.AgentToAnywhereConfig)
actionType: DENY
displayName: "Require Agent Gateway for Agent Engine egress"
description: "Block creation of Agent Engine instance if a Agent Gateway is not specified."
```

## Agent Gateway \- Authorization 

Agent Gateway supports customizable Authorization Policies that allow you to invoke authorization services like Identity-based access authorization and content security when processing requests or responses through the Agent Gateway. These can be authorization services from Google Cloud, from Google Cloud ISV partners, or even your own custom authorization services. 

Using Agent Gateway, an **enterprise administrator** is able to enforce access controls to MCP servers for agents running in their enterprise on Google Cloud. 

1. **Default Deny:** By default, access to any \[2P/3P\] remote MCP servers that have not been registered in Tools Registry is blocked. Admin can optionally choose to allow access to unregistered MCP servers and unregistered tools. 

2. **Selective Allow:** An **enterprise administrator** can selectively allow use of a specified  \[2P/3P\] remote MCP server:   
   1. At the org-, folder-, or project-level;  
   2. By environment, e.g., using tags from Tools Registry \[**POST-MVP**\]

3. **Granular Control:** An **enterprise administrator** can use Google Cloud IAM to grant and deny individual agents or clients access to MCP servers and tools based on the tool name, and whether the tool is read-only or read-write.

4. **Custom Authorization:** An **enterprise administrator** can insert their own custom authorization grant and deny individual agents or clients access to MCP servers and tools. 

Agent Gateway defines two categories of Authorization policies: 

1. **Request Authorization** (REQUEST\_AUTHZ) : These authorization policies provide Identity-based access authorization, allowing you to enforce fine-grained access permissions. By default, Agent Gateway uses Google Cloud’s Identity Aware Proxy as an authorization service for Identity-based access authorization for your agents.   
2. **Content Authorization** (CONTENT\_AUTHZ): These authorization policies provide content filtering and security for use-cases such as blocking prompt injection attacks, leaking sensitive/PII data or or serving harmful content. By default, Agent Gateway uses Google Cloud’s Model Armor as an authorization service for content security. 

### Agent Gateway – Request flow 

At runtime, the Agent Gateway dataplane does the following: 

* **(POST\_MVP**: Establishes authenticated and encrypted mTLS connectivity with agents using GCP’s Agent Identity, and TLS connectivity to destination resource as applicable)    
* **Request Authorization**: Authenticates client requests to determine client identity. For destinations in Agent Registry,looks up attributes and metadata for the destination (e.g. Semantic Tags — **POST\_MVP** and tools attributes \- Read-Only Tool). Uses request authorization extension service like Identity-Aware Proxy to enforce authorization policy based on client identity. The authorization policy can be defined in GCP IAM, or you can also use your own custom authorization policy.   
  * For requests to unknown MCP servers, the specified authorization policy for unknown MCP servers is enforced (e.g. DRY\_RUN).   
* **Content Authorization**: Sends traffic to the configured AI protection service like  [Google Cloud Model Armor](https://cloud.google.com/security-command-center/docs/model-armor-overview) for sanitization, or additional callouts to other services you have configured (e.g. custom Data Loss Prevention solution)   
* **Observability**: Generates logs for traffic with metadata about the client identity, tool call params, request authorization result, bytes, critical events.   All agent-agent and agent-MCP server interactions are captured in Cloud Audit Logs with associated metadata such as Client and Server ID simplified security investigations.  (OTel traces on roadmap)   
* 

![][image5]

Authorization policies can be consistently applied to an Agent Gateway for any deployment mode (self-managed or google-managed Agent Gateway) 

| Note: In this release, Authorization policies are not supported on the CLIENT\_TO\_AGENT path when using a Google-Managed Agent Gateway; Authorization policies can only be configured on the AGENT\_TO\_ANYWHERE path.  |
| :---- |

### Configuration of Authorization Extensions  

**Step 1: Create a service extension**

| $ cat \>extension1.yaml \<\<EOF name: extension1service: FQDNmetadata:   \# Optional failOpen: trueEOF |
| :---- |

Replace FQDN  with the following as needed: 

* **Google Cloud Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP)** :    iap.googleapis.com For IAP, also pass the following in the `metadata` field `‘iamEnforcementMode: DRY_RUN’` if you want IAP to not enforce policies but still log access requests.  
* **Google Cloud Model Armor**:  modelarmor.\<location\>.rep.googleapis.com  
  For Model Armor, also pass the following in the metadata field:  
  model\_armor\_settings: ‘\[{“response\_template\_id”:“TEMPLATE\_ID”}, {“request\_template\_id”:“TEMPLATE\_ID”}\]’  
  **Note**: [List available templates](https://docs.cloud.google.com/sdk/gcloud/reference/model-armor/templates/list) to get the required TemplateID  
* For an extension from a Google Cloud ISV partner or your own service, you can pass a fully qualified domain name that resolves to where the service is running. For Google-managed Agent Gateways, ensure that DNS peering is setup with the Google tenant project.

Use the `gcloud service-extensions authz-extensions` command to create the extension

| gcloud service-extensions authz-extensions import extension1 \\   \--source=extension1.yaml \\   \--location=\<location\> |
| :---- |

**Step 2: Apply the service extension to the Agent Gateway authorization profile** 

Next, create a `CUSTOM` authorization profile that delegates authorization to the extension setup in the previous step. 

| $ cat \>authz-profile1.yaml \<\<EOFname: my-authz-profile1target:  resources:  \- "//networkservices.googleapis.com/…/agentGateways/gateway1"policyProfile: REQUEST\_AUTHZ  action: CUSTOMcustomProvider: authzExtension: \- resources:   \- "projects/…/authzExtensions/extension1"EOF |
| :---- |

In policyProfile:

* Specify `REQUEST_AUTHZ` policy profile for extension  to allow/deny requests. (e.g. For Identity-Aware Proxy)   
* Specify `CONTENT_AUTHZ` policy profile for extensions to process the request & response body (e.g. for Model Armor)

Use the `gcloud network-security authz-policies` command to create the policy.

| gcloud network-security authz-policies import my-agentgw-authz \\  \--source=authz-policy.yaml \\  \--location=us-east1 |
| :---- |

#### **Authorization Policy \- Conditions** 

**Protocol-specific conditions:** 

Invoke Model Armor only for MCP Traffic: 

| $ cat \> my-conditional-authz-policy.yaml \<\<EOFname: conditional-extensiontarget:  resources:  \- "networkservices.googleapis.com/v1/projects/123/agentGateways/gateway1"policyProfile: CONTENT\_AUTHZ httpRules:\- to:    operations:    \- mcp: action: CUSTOM  customProvider: authzExtension:   resources:   \- networkservices.googleapis.com/v1/projects/123/authzExtensions/model-armorEOF |
| :---- |

**Registry Entry-specific conditions:** 

You can use the entries and attributes in the Agent Registry or attributes from agentic protocols to conditionally invoke Extensions. For example, say you want to specify that requests to Tool-B with read-write should go through a PANW extension. Assuming you have already created an extension “panw-ext”, define the following Authorization Policy:

| $ cat \> my-conditional-authz-policy.yaml \<\<EOFname: conditional-extensiontarget:  resources:  \- "networkservices.googleapis.com/v1/projects/123/agentGateways/gateway1"policyProfile: CONTENT\_AUTHZ httpRules:\- to: agentregistry.googleapis.com/projects/123/location/abc/Tools/id    operations:    \- mcp: action: CUSTOM  customProvider: authzExtension:   resources:   \- networkservices.googleapis.com/v1/projects/123/authzExtensions/panw-extEOF |
| :---- |

## Appendix 

### **REST Resource:  v1.projects.locations.agentGateway**

| Methods |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| [get](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1IkyrTlHS0Sb1x015xCItbAgRz5629mGV8NU_zQ_Uibk/edit?resourcekey=0-JhYyWbSnvsam0paq-9X1lQ&tab=t.5vlelzt31zkf#heading=h.f1vud2mcufry) | Get details of an AgentGateway. |
| list | List all agent gateways in a {project, location}. |
| import | Create an agent gateway based on the YAML definition (see below) of the Agent Gateway resource |
| update | Update an Agent Gateway with an updated resource definition  |
| delete | Delete an Agent Gateway resource |

Fields in schema to create an Agent Gateway:

| Field Name | Type | Required/Optional | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| name | string | Required | Required. The name for this resource. The name should conform to [RFC-1034](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc1034); which restricts to letters, numbers, and hyphens, with the first character a letter, the last a letter or a number, and a 63 character maximum. |
| protocols\[\] | object | Required | A list of agentic protocols for which to enable decoding. For MVP, ‘MCP’ will be the only supported protocol. |
| registries  | object | Optional | A list of API registries containing the agents, MCP servers and tools governed by the Agent Gateway.  Must be of format “//agentregistry.googleapis.com/projects/p/locations/” |
| deployment\_mode | object | Required | Deployment mode of the network proxy  \# GOOGLE\_MANAGED \-  Default – Proxy is orchestrated and managed in by GoogleCloud in a tenant project   \# SELF\_MANAGED \- Use existing supported Google Cloud Networking product (e.g. SWP, Load Balancer, etc.) |
| deployment\_mode.goverened\_access\_path | object | Optional | Operating Mode of Agent Gateway:  AGENT\_TO\_ANYWHERE \- Default – Govern agent outbound to destinations CLIENT\_TO\_AGENT  \- Protect inbound to Agent or Tool Note: Naming of fields is to be finalized |
| **`network_config.egress.network-attachment`** | object | Required | The PSC-Interface network attachment for agent’s outbound connectivity  |

### **ADK support** 

| from google.adk.governance import AgentGateway authz1 \= AgentGatewayAuthzExt( .... ) authz2 \= AgentGatewayAuthzExt( .... ) \# Instantiate the Agent Gateway wrapper agentGW \= AgentGateway(     name="my-agent-gateway",     protocols=\[MCP\],     governed\_access\_path=AGENT\_TO\_ANYWHERE,     registries="",     extensions=\[authz1, authz2\] ) |
| :---- |

### **Permissions** 

The following  permissions are associated with the AgentGateway resource:

- networkservices.agentGateways.create  
- networkservices.agentGateways.update  
- networkservices.agentGateways.delete  
- networkservices.agentGateways.get  
- networkservices.agentGateways.list  
- networkservices.agentGateways.use

To reference ForwardingRule and Gateway resources in self\_managed deployment mode, the user must have permissions in the corresponding project(s) for:

- compute.forwardingRules.update  
- networkservices.gateways.update

### **Permissions**

Permissions for **AgentGateway Admin** to create, update, get, list and delete agent gateway resources.

- compute.networkAttachments.list  
  - compute.regions.list  
  - modelarmor.templates.list  
  - modelarmor.templates.list  
  - networksecurity.authzPolicies.create  
  - networksecurity.authzPolicies.delete  
  - networksecurity.authzPolicies.get  
  - networksecurity.authzPolicies.list  
  - networksecurity.operations.get  
  - networkservices.agentGateways.create  
  - networkservices.agentGateways.delete  
  - networkservices.agentGateways.get  
  - networkservices.agentGateways.list  
  - networkservices.agentGateways.update  
  - networkservices.agentGateways.use  
  - networkservices.authzExtensions.create  
  - networkservices.authzExtensions.delete  
  - networkservices.authzExtensions.get  
  - networkservices.authzExtensions.list  
  - networkservices.authzExtensions.update  
  - networkservices.authzExtensions.use  
  - Networkservices.operations.get

Other required roles for Agent Platform administration: 

- `roles/iap.admin`, `roles/agentregistry.viewer`, `roles/resourcemanager.projectIamAdmin`, `roles/networksecurity.admin`, `roles/serviceextensions.admin, roles/iap.settingsAdmin`

  - `roles/logging.viewer, roles/logging.privateLogViewer`

  - `Modelarmor.callouts.invoke, modelarmor.locations.*, modelarmor.templates.*`

  - `Resourcemanager.projects.get, resourcemanager.projects.list`

### 

### **Supported Regions (MVP)**  

* asia-east1  
* asia-east2  
* asia-northeast1  
* asia-northeast3  
* asia-south1  
* asia-southeast1  
* asia-southeast2  
* australia-southeast2  
* europe-southwest1  
* Europe-west1 → Used by GE-EU  
* europe-west2  
* europe-west3  
* europe-west4  
* europe-west6  
* europe-west8  
* northamerica-northeast2  
* southamerica-east1  
* Us-central1  → Used by GE (Global) and GE-US  
* us-east4  
* us-west1

### **Agent Gateway in GKE** 

See:    
[go/gke-diy-agent-gw-dev-entry](http://go/gke-diy-agent-gw-dev-entry)  
[go/gke-diy-agent-cloud-next26](http://go/gke-diy-agent-cloud-next26)  
[go/agentgateway-cn-gke-diy-phase1-ug](http://goto.google.com/agentgateway-cn-gke-diy-phase1-ug) 

### **Agent Gateway Backlog**


| Item | Public Preview Limitation | GA-Blocker  | Roadmap enhancement |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Self-signed (PrivateCA) destinations** | Agent Gateway cannot connect to destinations with self-signed certifications.  **Impact – EXTREMELY HIGH**: **Core use-case functionality** – customers are not able to connect to internal MCP servers/tools that usually have self-signed certificates.  | **Yes** | SWP will be enhanced to support connectivity to destinations with self-signed certificates.  No additional customer config/updates needed. |
| **Egress governance for agents outside GCP** | Agent Gateway can only enforce governance for agents on GCP (AgentEngine/GeminiEnterprise)  **Impact \- HIGH**: **Core use-case functionality** – customers cannot use Agent Gateway to govern agents running on-prem or 3P clouds  | **Yes** | AgentGateway (Google-managed)  will be accessible via a public DNS endpoint using NLB/External-Proxy-LB; allowing . This will allow governed access for agents outside GCP, assuming the Identity of the agent is federated into GCP.   |
| **Simplified Agent Gateway setup \- PSC automation**  | When customers create Agent Gateway, they have to delve into network complexity – specifically PSC-I network attachment details – to connect to VPC destinations.  **Impact \- HIGH: User experience**  | **Yes** | PSC automation will allow Agent Gateway to connect automatically to registered destinations, including destinations in VPCs, without requiring customers to specify PSC-I network attachments.  |
| **Simplified Agent Gateway setup \- DNS service binding** | When customers create Agent Gateway, they have to delve into network complexity – specifically DNS peering configurations – to connect to VPC destinations.  **Impact \- HIGH: User experience**  | **Yes** | DNS service binding will allow Agent Gateway to resolve destinations of registered targets in VPCs, without requiring customers to specify DNS peering configurations. |
| **AgentGateway Observability dashboards**  | Customers have to themselves build dashboards to track \# of Authz success / denys from the authz logs.   **Impact \- HIGH: User experience** | **Yes** | Out of box observability dashboards for AgentGW to indicate key indicators on  of Authz success / denys , enforcement of policies (by Policy & by Client)  |
| **Governance for vibe-coding clients  w/ device-local enforcement** | Agent Gateway only governs agents on AgentEngine/Gemini.  **Impact \- Medium**: Core use-case functionality – customers cannot use Agent Gateway to govern agentic clients (e.g. Cursor, GeminiCLI, Claude Desktop, etc.)   | No | A new project (Mobile Envoy integrated into LiteLLM) will extend Agent Gateway’s security and governance functionality to agentic clients like GeminiCLI, Cursor, ClaudeCode, etc. on user local devices  |
| **Ingress using IAP**  | Agent Gateway on Ingress only uses IAM policies  | No | AgentGateway on Ingress for AE/GE will support IAP based access authorization policies.  |
| **Access Policies on User \+ Agent Identity** | Access policies are only defined and enforced on Agent Identity  **Impact**: Customers cannot use Agent Gateway to enforce controls based on the User-Identity  | No | AgentGateway on Ingress for AE/GE will support IAP based access authorization policies based on User \+ Agent Identity.  The User Identity could be obtained from authentication with 3P IdPs like Okta, Ping, etc.  The User+Agent Identity context is passed in the Agent-\>Agent or Agent-\>Tools calls, so that the final tool call or agent access authorization can take the user+agent context into account.  |
| **Custom extensibility for 2P/3P at Ingress** |  | No |  |
| **Registry \-- Registry attributes (E.g. Tags)** |  | No |  |
| **Cross-Project registry references** |  | No |  |
| **Trace spans** |  | No |  |
| **API quotas \- based on User+Agent Identity** |  | No |  |
| **Token quotas – based on User+Agent Identity** |  | No |  |
| **SvcExtensions Producer/Consumer separation** |  | No |  |
| **Simplified API for default Agent Gateway configuration** |  | No |  |
| **CRM hierarchy support for Agent Gateway policies** |  | No |  |
|  |  | No |  |
